notes: RLHF operationalizes risk governance by embedding human preferences into model training as a measurable alignment method.
Human feedback training directly reduces harmful bias and dangerous content generation by penalizing unsafe outputs.
RLHF is a core operational control for AI system alignment during model development.
Human preference training reduces discriminatory outputs and improves human-AI interaction quality.
DPO provides a direct optimization method for aligning model outputs with human preferences without a separate reward model.
Direct preference optimization reduces bias and harmful content by training directly on human preference pairs.
DPO is an operational alignment technique applied during the model development lifecycle.
Preference optimization directly targets reduction of discriminatory and harmful outputs.
Constitutional AI encodes explicit safety principles into training, supporting governance-by-design and measurable risk management.
Principle-based self-critique directly reduces dangerous content, bias, and obscene/abusive outputs.
Constitutional principles define leadership-level safety policies operationalized through the training process.
Self-critique based on explicit principles reduces discrimination, malicious use potential, and harmful interaction patterns.
Principle-based training supports the EU AI Act requirement for high-risk systems to have appropriate risk management measures.
Adversarial training improves model robustness as part of risk measurement and management processes.
Training on adversarial examples hardens models against dangerous content elicitation and information security attacks.
Adversarial training improves resistance to prompt injection attacks and training data poisoning.
Hardening through adversarial examples defends against initial ML access, defense evasion, and attack staging.
Robustness training reduces the effectiveness of adversarial attacks intended for malicious use.
Safety-specific reward models provide measurable signals for risk reduction during alignment training.
Dedicated safety reward signals penalize dangerous, biased, and abusive content generation.
Safety reward modeling is an operational control ensuring safety objectives are optimized during training.
Harmlessness reward signals reduce discriminatory outputs and content that enables malicious use.
Refusal training implements risk management by teaching models to decline unsafe requests.
Trained refusal directly blocks CBRN information, dangerous content, and obscene/abusive outputs.
Refusal mechanisms defend against prompt injection attempts and reduce insecure output generation.
Abstention training prevents disclosure of information hazards and resists malicious use attempts.
Refusal capability supports the requirement for high-risk AI systems to include appropriate risk mitigation measures.
Multimodal alignment maps and measures safety risks specific to cross-modal inputs and outputs.
Visual and audio safety alignment reduces dangerous, obscene, and biased content across modalities.
Cross-modal safety training addresses discrimination in image generation and prevents malicious visual content creation.
Multimodal alignment extends operational safety controls to cover non-text modalities.
Bias mitigation directly maps, measures, and manages fairness risks identified in the RMF.
Post-training bias reduction directly addresses the harmful bias and homogenization risk profile.
Bias mitigation satisfies the EU AI Act requirement for high-risk systems to minimize discriminatory outcomes.
Bias mitigation serves as both an operational control and a performance evaluation metric.
De-biasing techniques directly target discrimination, exclusion, and toxicity harms.
Unlearning supports risk management by enabling removal of problematic data influence from deployed models.
Machine unlearning addresses data privacy risks by removing specific training data influence from model weights.
Unlearning supports GDPR right-to-erasure compliance required for high-risk AI systems processing personal data.
Targeted forgetting can address information hazards embedded in model weights from training data.
Data quality filtering addresses upstream risk mapping by ensuring training corpus quality and reducing known hazards.
Filtering training data reduces privacy risks, bias from low-quality sources, and intellectual property exposure.
Quality filtering defends against training data poisoning by removing malicious or corrupted data.
Data quality filtering is a support resource and operational control for responsible AI development.
Filtering defends against ML attack staging by detecting anomalous or poisoned training samples.
Dataset auditing maps and measures representational risks in training data as required by the RMF.
Auditing training data for demographic biases and privacy issues mitigates harmful bias and data privacy risks.
Dataset auditing satisfies EU AI Act requirements for data governance including examining training data for biases.
Dataset documentation provides support resources, operational controls, and performance evaluation baselines.
Representation analysis identifies discriminatory biases and socioeconomic imbalances in training data.
Red teaming is a primary measurement and management method for identifying AI system risks.
Adversarial testing probes for CBRN risks, dangerous content generation, and information security vulnerabilities.
Red teaming identifies prompt injection, insecure output, and sensitive information disclosure vulnerabilities.
Systematic adversarial testing identifies weaknesses across initial access, model access, defense evasion, and attack staging.
Red teaming satisfies EU AI Act requirements for adversarial testing and robustness validation of high-risk systems.
Red teaming serves as both an operational validation method and a performance evaluation tool.
Adversarial testing probes across discrimination, information hazards, misinformation, and malicious use categories.
Safety benchmarks provide standardized measurement of AI risks and enable tracking over time.
Benchmark suites quantify CBRN, confabulation, dangerous content, and bias risks with standardized metrics.
Standardized benchmarking satisfies EU AI Act requirements for testing and validation of high-risk systems.
Safety benchmarks are a primary method for performance evaluation under ISO 42001.
Benchmarks provide quantitative measurement across discrimination, information hazard, misinformation, and malicious use harm categories.
Community evaluation supports diverse stakeholder measurement of AI system risks.
External community evaluation provides feedback on human-AI interaction quality and configuration risks.
Community evaluation contributes to performance evaluation and drives continual improvement.
Community testing surfaces human-computer interaction harms that internal testing may miss.
Input guardrails are a core risk management control for filtering harmful inputs at runtime.
Input classifiers block CBRN queries, dangerous content requests, security attacks, and abusive prompts.
Input guardrails are the primary defense against prompt injection attacks.
Input filtering defends against initial access and ML model access attack tactics.
Input classification blocks prompts designed for malicious use of the model.
Input guardrails contribute to the risk management system required for high-risk AI applications.
System prompts implement organizational safety policies as runtime behavioral constraints.
Metaprompts directly configure human-AI interaction boundaries and behavioral constraints.
System prompts establish behavioral boundaries that defend against prompt injection and excessive agency.
System prompts are an operational control that enforces safety policies at inference time.
Output filtering is a core risk management control for blocking unsafe model generations.
Post-generation filters block dangerous, obscene/abusive, and biased content before it reaches users.
Output safety systems directly defend against insecure output handling by filtering harmful generations.
Output filtering catches discriminatory, information-hazard, and malicious content that bypasses training-time alignment.
Output moderation contributes to the accuracy and robustness requirements for high-risk AI systems.

Grounding mechanisms directly mitigate confabulation and information integrity risks.
Factuality enforcement reduces the risk of user overreliance on fabricated model outputs.

Defense-in-depth architecture implements layered risk management as recommended by the RMF.
Cascaded safety checks provide redundant mitigation of dangerous content and information security risks.
Multi-stage pipelines provide layered defense against both prompt injection and insecure output.

Configurable policies support organizational governance customization and adaptive risk management.
Adjustable safety thresholds address human-AI configuration risks by giving appropriate control to operators.
Configurable policies are an operational control allowing context-appropriate safety tuning.

Injection defense addresses specific security risks identified through risk measurement.

Injection defense guards against adversarial initial access, model access exploitation, and defense evasion tactics.
Jailbreak defense prevents adversaries from weaponizing models for malicious use through prompt manipulation.
Sandboxing manages the risk of model-generated code causing unintended harm to production systems.

Sandboxing defends against insecure output handling, insecure plugin design, and excessive agency from code execution.
Containerized execution defends against adversarial code execution and privilege escalation.
Sandboxing prevents malicious use of model-generated code to compromise systems.
